facebook.content_angle: Pacing strategy — how most club swimmers go out too hard in the first 50m and blow up, versus the negative split approach elite swimmers use to finish faster than they start
facebook.post_text:
Going out hard feels fast. It's costing you the race. 🔥

Most club swimmers hit the first 50m on adrenaline and arrive at the wall cooked — watching their time fall apart in the back half.

Elite swimmers don't race that way. Here's why:

👉 The first 50m should feel controlled, not maxed — you're building, not burning
👉 A negative split means your second half is faster than your first — that's where races are won
👉 Your aerobic engine takes 30–40 seconds to properly engage — go out at 95% and let it catch up

The swimmers who look effortless in the final 25m? They were smarter at the start. 💡

✔️ Practice negative splits in training — not just races
✔️ Use a tempo tool or count strokes to stay honest in the first half
✔️ Film your splits. If your back half is slower, your pacing is the problem

What does your race strategy actually look like right now — are you a fast-starter or a strong finisher?
facebook.engagement_bait: Drop a 🔥 if you've blown up in the back half before — we've all been there.
facebook.call_to_action: What does your race strategy actually look like right now — are you a fast-starter or a strong finisher?
